# Ubiquitous Language

## Statement

Every term has exactly one meaning system-wide, enforced through a living
glossary that all governed entities reference.

## Rationale

Ambiguity is the root cause of most organizational miscommunication. When
"component" means one thing in engineering and another in finance, every
cross-domain conversation starts from confusion. People think they agree when
they're actually talking about different things. This isn't a minor annoyance —
it's a structural failure mode that compounds with scale.

A ubiquitous language (term from Domain-Driven Design) eliminates this by
establishing single definitions that everyone uses. The glossary isn't
documentation — it's infrastructure. It's the shared substrate that makes all
other communication possible. Without it, every standard, rule, and principle is
subject to interpretation drift.

This is especially critical in AI-heavy environments where LLMs interpret terms
literally. An ambiguous term in a prompt or governance document produces
unpredictable AI behavior. Terms that lack clear definitions should be flagged
and resolved, not left ambiguous and hoped-for-the-best.

## In Practice

JOS maintains a 595+ term glossary with axis tagging. Every governed component uses
terms from this glossary. New terms get formal definitions before use in
standards or rules. Cross-venture terms are resolved at JOS level, not reinvented
per venture.

When a new concept enters the system, it receives a glossary entry before it
receives a governance object. When existing terms are found to be ambiguous
(meaning different things in different contexts), they are either split into
distinct terms or given a single canonical definition with explicit notes about
former usage.

The glossary is living — it grows with the system and is subject to the same
governance lifecycle as any other JOS object.

## Connects To

- JOS-C06 (Documentation Discipline)
- JOS-C14 (Ambient Discoverability)
- JOS-SO-005 (Registry is structural truth)

promoted to JOS as venture-agnostic.
